coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensing of mercury ions in electrolyte solutions by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot electrodes at ultralow concentrations
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Chun-Chieh
Hsieh, Chien-Te
Juang, Ruey-Shin
Gu, Siyong
Ashraf Gandomi, Yasser
Kelly, Ryan E.
Kihm, Kenneth D.
coredata.dc:description: Electrochemical detection of mercury ions in aqueous solution was investigated at indium tin oxide (ITO) conducting glass electrode modified by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-doped GQDs). The N-doped GQDs with an average particle size of 4.5 nm were synthesized through an infrared-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The GQD sample contains high oxidation and amidation level, i.e., O/C and N/C atomic ratios: 37.6% and 30.7%, respectively. The electrochemical sensing toward Hg2+ ions was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Based on the CV and EIS analyses, both the reductive and oxidative peak currents as well as the equivalent series resistance demonstrate a decreasing trend with increased Hg2+ concentration. The detection limit of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes toward Hg2+ ions reached 10 ppb with the accumulation time of 32 s. The GQD/ITO electrodes also exhibit superior selectivity toward the target contaminant (i.e. Hg2+ ion). Accordingly, the functionalized GQDs pave the way for engineering the electrochemical sensors capable of detecting toxic Hg2+ ions with superb sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen doping
Mercury ions
Electrolyte solution
Infrared-assisted synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GQDs by IR-assisted method
Physiochemical and optical properties of GQDs
Electrochemical detection of Hg2+ on GQD electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Republic of China Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS (a) C1s and (b) O1s spectra of N-doped GQD sample, deconvoluted by a multiple Gaussian function.
(a) TEM micrograph of N-doped GQD sample and (b) SEM image of N-GQDs on ITO conducting glass. The inset of Fig. 2(a) shows the lattice fringes of N-doped GQD particles.
Photographs of N-doped GQD/ITO electrode (a) before and (b) after the electrochemical detection under UV irradiation. The inset of Fig. 3(a) shows the N-doped GQD suspension under UV irradiation.
(a) Cyclic voltammetry profiles of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes at different Hg2+ concentrations with a scan rate of 50 mV s− 1. (b) The linearity plots of peak current versus concentration.
Schematic diagram illustrating the electrochemical detection on the GQD/ITO electrode: (a) ionic adsorption and (b) electrochemical redox reaction.
XPS (a) Hg 4f and (b) O 1s spectra of N-doped GQD sample after the electrochemical detection of mercury ions.

(a) Nyquist plots of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes at different Hg2+ concentrations. (b) The linearity plots of equivalent series resistance versus concentration. The inset of Fig. 7(b) shows the proposed equivalent circuit for correlating the electrochemical impedance behavior.
(a) Selectivity test and (b) signal ratios of N-doped GQD/ITO electrode after sequent adding metal ions.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensing of mercury ions in electrolyte solutions by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot electrodes at ultralow concentrations

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrochemical detection of mercury ions in aqueous solution was investigated at indium tin oxide (ITO) conducting glass electrode modified by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-doped GQDs). The N-doped GQDs with an average particle size of 4.5 nm were synthesized through an infrared-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The GQD sample contains high oxidation and amidation level, i.e., O/C and N/C atomic ratios: 37.6% and 30.7%, respectively. The electrochemical sensing toward Hg2+ ions was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Based on the CV and EIS analyses, both the reductive and oxidative peak currents as well as the equivalent series resistance demonstrate a decreasing trend with increased Hg2+ concentration. The detection limit of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes toward Hg2+ ions reached 10 ppb with the accumulation time of 32 s. The GQD/ITO electrodes also exhibit superior selectivity toward the target contaminant (i.e. Hg2+ ion). Accordingly, the functionalized GQDs pave the way for engineering the electrochemical sensors capable of detecting toxic Hg2+ ions with superb sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals found in the industrial wastewater streams are very harmful to human health and the environment [1,2]. Bioaccumulation of the mercury ions (Hg2+), one of the most toxic heavy metal ions, in aquatic organisms through the food chain increases risk for many severe diseases such as neurological disorders, gastrointestinal perforation, and even renal failure [3–5]. High concentration of Hg2+ induces pulmonary function impairment, kidney failure, chest pain, and dyspnea [6,7]. Therefore, it is of significant importance to selectively and effectively remove heavy metal ions form the water stream. Several prior works have been devoted for removing these toxic ions from the aqueous solutions; chemical precipitation [8], ion exchange [9], flocculation [10], electrolysis [11], membrane-based separation [12], adsorption [13–15] and photocatalysis [16] are among these techniques. Although a relatively high removal efficiency can be achieved via implementing these methods, the detection limit is usually too high for separating the pollutants at ultra-low concentrations. It is important to note that the maximum permissible concentration of Hg2+ ions, one of the Priority Pollutants listed by United States Environmental Protection Agency, for discharge into inland surface waters and drinking water is <10 and 1 ppb, respectively (tolerance limit) [17]. As a result, there is a significant need for developing techniques capable of detecting the Hg2+ ions at ultra-low concentration (i.e. permissible limit for the drinking water).

Generally, the oxidized form of Hg2+ ions is the major source of contamination; thus, a reliable, inexpensive, and selective approach enabling a “ppb-ppm” level detection is needed. As compared to the conventional chromatography/mass spectrometry and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [18], electrochemical methods are greatly advantageous due to high sensitivity, selectivity, low-cost, and ease of operation [19]. However, to avoid false signals, the selection of electrochemical sensing materials for a selective detection is of crucial importance. Several previous works have reported on the detection of Hg2+ ions using Rhodamine derivative [20], bismuth hexagons [21], thiolated graphene [22], and gold nanoparticles [23] as the sensing electrodes. Recently, we developed a novel technique for detecting mercury ions in aqueous solution with high selectivity and sensitivity via fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [24]. Despite having high selectivity and sensitivity, requiring an optical spectrometer for measuring the concentration of Hg2+ ions, further limits the implementation of this technique. Therefore, there is a substantial need for developing some alternative methods capable of selectively detecting the mercury ions at ultra-low concentration.

In this work, we have employed GQDs (with an average particle size ~4.5 nm) as the sensing electrodes for electrochemically detecting the Hg2+ ions. GQDs, as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have already been used for a variety of applications including light-emitting diodes [25], optical detectors [26], bio-imaging probes [27], and visible-light-driven photocatalysts [28]. The GQDs can be synthesized using a graphene-based precursor or through carbonization of a hydrocarbon precursor [24]. Recently, doping graphene with heteroatoms, such as electron-rich nitrogen (N), has been introduced for altering the chemical properties as well as the band-gap of graphene structures and subsequently inducing p-type or n-type conductivity [29–31]. The incorporation of N atoms within the GQD structure facilitates the quantum yields [32] and improves the electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction [33,34]. Several prior efforts have been devoted for synthesizing amino-functionalized carbon nanostructures; the hydrothermal route in the presence of NH4OH [30], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) with pyridine [35], and pyrolysis of citric acid-ethanolamine precursor [36] are some examples. These synthesis routes, although being very effective, are not capable of synthesizing carbon nanostructure with high amidation level (i.e. N/C atomic ratio >30%).

Accordingly, this work introduces a high-throughput heating system with superior energy efficiency, for large-scale production of N-doped GQDs with ultra-high amidation level. An induction heating technique was innovated to synthesize GQDs via the pyrolysis of carbon precursors under infrared (IR) irradiation. The as-prepared GQDs were employed as the electrode material for selectively detecting the mercury ions with high sensitivity. The zero-dimensional GQDs were also uniformly coated onto indium tin oxide (ITO) conducting glass, forming GQD/ITO hybrid electrode nanostructure. The robust design of GQD-based sensors paves the way for employing GQDs as the sensing electrodes for detecting various types of pollutants or bio-species at ultra-low concentration. The simplicity and scalability of the GQDs design enables real-time monitoring of heavy metals in large and industrial scales.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs by IR-assisted method
ce:section.ce:para: The IR-assisted induction heating method was employed for controlling the growth rate as well as the nanostructure of the GQDs. First, two precursors, citric acid (C6H8O7, molecular weight: 180, purity: 99%) and urea (CON2H4, molecular weight: 60, purity: 99%), were homogeneously mixed in a 2D mixer (with zirconia balls) at 500 rpm for 30 min. The weight ratio of citric acid to urea was set at 1:1. The mixing powder (ca. 10 g) was poured into a thermally resistive glass pan (Pyrix glass) and subsequently was placed in a home-made IR furnace. Six medium-wave IR heaters with near-IR wavelength range of 1.4–3.2 μm were utilized in the IR furnace. The heating filament was a metallic wire (with a maximum power density of 80 kW m− 2) along with the quartz tube as the insulating/protective cover. Upon placing the GQD samples inside the furnace, the IR treatment was conducted in three consecutive steps: (i) ramping the temperature from ambient temperature to 250 °C at the rate of 10 °C min− 1, (ii) maintaining the temperature at 250 °C for 10 min (isothermal process), and (iii) cooling down the furnace from 250 °C to the ambient temperature. These three steps were carried out in air without any other flowing gas. The GQD samples were subsequently dispersed and washed in distilled water. The samples were finally centrifuged and collected at 15,000 rpm for 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical and optical properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology as well as surface structure of the GQD samples was investigated via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU-8200) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Talos F200s). An X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu Labx XRD-6000), equipped with a Cu–Kα emitter, was used to characterize the crystallinity. The bonding structure of GQD samples was analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw Micro-Raman spectrometer). The chemical composition of the GQD sample was characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra were recorded using a Fison VG ESCA210 spectrometer equipped with Mg–Kα radiation. The XPS C 1s, Hg 4f, and O 1s spectra of GQD samples were deconvoluted implementing a non-linear least square optimization algorithm with a symmetric Gaussian function. The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the GQD samples were also analyzed. The GQD suspension was ultrasonically dispersed in distilled water at ambient temperature to ensure a uniform dispersion. It is important to note that the GQD suspension was very stable due to superior hydrophilicity. The PL emission spectra of each suspension were acquired using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Hg2+ on GQD electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: A drop-coating method was used to prepare GQD electrodes for non-enzymatic sensing toward mercury ions in the liquid phase. Herein 25 mg of the GQD powder was dispersed in a solution containing 6.5 mL of distilled water and 0.9 mL of Nafion 117 solution (Aldrich; 5 wt%). The mixture was ultra-sonicated for 0.5 h to form a uniform paste. A sequential dropping (layer-by-layer) process was performed on an ITO conducting glass to form a sensing electrode. The ITO substrates with an exposed area of 3 × 3 cm2 were cleaned by sonication in ethanol and deionized water prior to the GQD coating. Afterward, the GQD/ITO hybrid electrodes were dehydrated at 80 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. The electrochemical sensing of mercury ions was conducted in a three-electrode configuration system. A Pt sheet and an Ag/AgCl electrode (in 3 M KCl aqueous solution) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The sensitivity of the electrodes were assessed with cyclic voltammetry (CV) as well as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using the Autolab PGSTAT 302 N potentiostat. To perform the CV measurements, various electrolyte configurations with different Hg2+ concentrations and 1 M NaOH were prepared and the scan rate of 50 mV s− 1 was implemented. The EIS analysis was carried out at −0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl within the frequency range of 100 kHz to 1 mHz. A digital camera was used to photograph the GQD-coated electrodes under ultraviolet (UV) irradiation (wavelength: 360 nm). To probe the selectivity, the sensor's response was analyzed versus other similar metallic ions co-existing within the solution. The metal ions including Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ were used for the selectivity analysis where 1 ppm of these ions (one at a time) was added into the electrolyte containing 1 ppm Hg2+ solution. The electrochemical analysis was subsequently conducted for these set of mixed metallic ion electrolytes.

The HR-TEM micrographs of the GQDs synthesized using the IR-assisted pyrolysis technique are shown in Fig. 1(a). As illustrated in Fig. 1, the GQDs have been homogeneously dispersed within the electrode structure and the particle size distribution is relatively uniform. The inset of Fig. 1(a) shows an elliptic-shape GQD nanostructure, confirming crystallinity with a lattice distance of ca. 0.21–0.22 nm along the (1120) lattice fringes of the graphene sheets [37]. The particle size of the elliptic GQDs ranges from 3 to 6 nm, i.e., average particle size: ca. 4.5 nm. The circular peripheries is commonly attributed to the thermodynamically favoured structure formed in the equilibrium state to minimize the edge free energies through atomic migration, rearrangement of atoms (e.g., C, N, O), and reconstruction of crystallization [38,39]. The SEM image of hybrid GQD/ITO electrode is provided in Fig. 1(b), demonstrating a series of GQD islands structured over the ITO substrate. As shown in the inset of Fig. 1(b), the GQDs dispersed within the electrode structure form a roughened surface promoting the electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions.

Typical XRD patterns of the N-doped GQD samples are provided in the
Electronic Supporting Information
(see Fig. S1(a)). According to Fig. S1, a distinct (002) diffraction peak can be identified at ~27.0°. Using the Bragg's law, we quantified the d (002) interlayer spacing distance as 3.30 Å. The d (002)-interlayer spacing distance of a defect-free graphite has been reported within the range of 0.335–0.340 nm [40] which is slightly greater than that of N-doped GQD samples. The smaller d (002)-interlayer distance can be attributed to the N dopant in the layered graphite structure reducing the interlayer spacing distance due to decreased atomic structure [41]. Of note, the intensity of the d (002) major peak reveals highly crystalline structure of GQDs (JCPDS Card No.: 41-1487) prepared from the IR-assisted synthesis technique. Typical Raman spectrum of GQD samples, as illustrated in Fig. S1(b), displays two characteristic peaks occurring at 1350 and 1580 cm− 1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The D band at 1350 cm− 1 is ascribed to amorphous carbon or deformation vibrations of a hexagonal ring, whereas the G band observed at 1580 cm− 1 is assigned to a single crystallite of graphite [42,43]. It is important to note that the Raman intensity ratio of D to G bands (I D/I G) is commonly used as a critical index for assessing the graphitic degree of carbon-based materials. The I D/I G of N-doped GQD samples is ~0.96, revealing the presence of a mixed atomic structure including amorphous carbon and crystalline graphite. This unique crystallinity is due to the existence of N and O decorations or N doping, the vibration of carbon atoms with dangling bonds within the crystal lattice plane, terminations of disordered graphite, and the defects in curved graphene nanosheets [41]. The position and shape of the 2D peak can be used to determine the number of graphene layers formed in the atomic structure [44]. Analyzing the Raman spectra at 514 nm, the number of graphene layers within the GQD hybrid electrode can be approximated in the range of 6–10.

XPS measurement was adopted to investigate the chemical compositions as well as the surface functional groups on the N-doped GQD hybrid electrodes. Based on the survey-scan spectra, the GQD samples were composed of three elements: C 1s (ca. 284 eV), N 1s (ca. 400 eV) and O 1s (ca. 532 eV) [45]. The GQDs also contained superior oxidation and amidation level where the O/C and N/C atomic ratios within the atomic structure were 37.6% and 30.7%, respectively. The ultrahigh amidation level achieved in this work (i.e. 30.7%) is the highest value reported in the literature (to the best of the authors' knowledge at the time of drafting this paper) for the GQD electrodes. Previous studies have reported GQD amidation level within the range of 5–15% where this moderate N/C ratio was primarily due to the formation of pyridinic or pyrrolic groups [46]. This finding reveals that the N-doped GQD hybrid electrodes possess a high degree of surface heterogeneity induced via large amount of O-/N-decoration or N doping on or into the zero-dimensional graphitic structure. To further explore the surface heterogeneity, the functional groups distribution was analyzed via decomposition of C 1s and N 1s peaks using the multiple Gaussian function. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the C 1s spectrum is decomposed into five peaks at 284.5 eV (CC or CC), 285.8 eV (CN), 286.6 eV (C–OH), 287.2 eV (C=O), and 288.6 eV (O–C=O), based on the atomic bond structure [45]. Herein, the urea precursor is the main source of nitrogen content with the CN group as the major functional group in the atomic structure. Considering the oxygen functional groups, phenolic (C–OH) or epoxy (C–O–C) group, due to strong electrostatic interactions, serve as active sites for adsorbing metal ions within the electrolyte. The presence of CF2 group can be attributed to the introduction of Nafion binder into the GQD/ITO electrodes. The N 1s spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 2(b), can be deconvoluted into three components including the predominant pyridinic N (ca. 399.2 eV), pyrrolic N (ca. 400.2 eV) and quaternary N (ca. 401.6 eV) [45]. It is generally recognized that the pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen are presumably due to the formation of aromatic C=N–C where N atom tends to bond with two carbon atoms or pyridine moiety, whereas the quaternary N is ascribed to the tertiary N–(C)3 where N atom is bonded to three sp2 carbon atoms, i.e., graphitic N [47]. Thus, the N-doped GQDs contain a large number of “lattice N" atoms, composed of in-plane N substitution (i.e., quaternary N), and pyrrolic/pyridinic N. As a result, it can be concluded that the IR-assisted pyrolysis is capable of synthesizing N-rich GQDs via in-situ doping steps (e.g., N substitution).

The photographs of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes before and after exposure to the Hg2+ ions are shown in Fig. 3 . Under the UV illumination (at 360 nm) the electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions using GQD/ITO hybrid electrodes can be clearly confirmed (compare Fig. 3(a) with Fig. 3(b)). As illustrated in Fig. 3, the fresh electrode emits bright green light under the UV illumination. The inset of Fig. 3(a) shows a green illumination from the N-doped GQD suspension where the GQDs were uniformly dispersed in distilled water (concentration: 100 ppm). The PL emission spectrum from the N-doped GQD suspension, as provided in Fig. S2, displays a quasi-symmetric peak ranging from 450 to 600 nm, confirming the green light emission at ca. 510 nm. It is important to note that the fluorescence from the N-doped GQD/ITO electrode is very sensitive and turns dark upon exposure to Hg2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 3(b). Due to the formation of a GQD–(Hg2+) x complex on the carbon surface, the PL quenching of N-doped GQDs is expected [48]. Thus, the surface intermediates (i.e. the GQD–(Hg2+) x complex) are capable of hindering the photon rejection from the highest occupied molecular orbital gap to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital gap, imparting the PL quenching behavior [24]. Due to the dramatic color change induced by partial surface coverage of GQDs nanostructure with Hg2+ ions, electrochemically detecting the mercury ions becomes feasible.

Typical CV measurements were employed to analyze the sensitivity of the electrochemical detection toward mercury ions using the N-doped hybrid GQD/ITO electrodes. The CV analysis was carried out in 1 M NaOH at different Hg2+ concentrations with a scan rate of 50 mV s− 1. A redox pair including reduction and oxidation peak at ca. 0.25 and 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl was observed, as shown in Fig. 4(a). As illustrated in Fig. 4, the oxidative/reductive peak current demonstrates an increasing trend with increased Hg2+ concentration, thus, the peak current can be calibrated and the resulting signal can be utilized for detecting/quantifying the mercury ions within the electrolyte. To probe the sensitivity, the variation of reduction/oxidation peak current with Hg2+ concentration was measured (see Fig. 4(b)). As shown in Fig. 4(b), a liner trendline was used for fitting the data (oxidation/reduction peak current versus Hg2+ concentration) for the mercury ions concentration in the ranges of 10–50 ppb (i.e., 0.05–0.25 μM). The correlation factors are found to be 0.998 and 0.997 for the linear plots of reduction and oxidation, respectively. The slope of reduction current is 0.219 μA cm− 2 ppb− 1, higher than that of oxidation current (i.e. 0.155 μA cm− 2 ppb− 1). Based on three times of the background noise, the limit of detection was 10 ppb with the accumulation time of 32 s, determined by the scan rate in the entire potential window. The detection limit by the electrochemical method is very close to that by fluorescence-quenching technique [24,49]. Basically, both methods offer excellent sensitivity toward Hg2+ detection in ppb-ppm level. However, the electrochemical detection enables simple, efficient, and low-cost, as compared to fluorescent quenching. Based on Fig. 4(b), a remarkable sensitivity along with ultra-fast response time was confirmed for the detection of mercury ions using GQD hybrid nanoelectrodes. Also, a superb detection limit was achieved that very well covers the requirement demanded by the World Health Organization for the maximum allowable level of mercury ions in drinking water (i.e. 1 ppb) [50].

Comparing the oxidation and reduction current response, the sensitivity of the reductive current to the variations in the mercury ion concentration (i.e. the slope of trendline in Fig. 4(b)) is about 1.41 times higher than that of the oxidative peak current. Therefore, the reduction peak current is a reliable signal for assessing the variations in the Hg2+ concentration. It can also be inferred that the metallic Hg0 has higher tendency for covering the GQD surface due to an irreversible redox reaction taking place at high ionic concentration. It is also important to elaborate the Hg2+ ions detection mechanism with the GQD hybrid electrodes. Fig. 5 schematically illustrates the two-step electrochemical detection pathway on the GQD electrode surface including (i) ionic adsorption and (ii) electrochemical redox reaction. In step (i), three consecutive pathways to form GQD*–Hg2+ complex can be formulated (see Fig. S3). In path (a), the surface reaction resulting in the formation of Hg-intermediates on the oxidized carbon surface can be formulated as [49,51,52]:
(R1)
GQD ∗ +
Hg
2 +
↔ GQD ∗ –
Hg
2 +

In path (b), the adsorptive sites for adsorbing Hg2+ ions are formed due to the presence of amide-carbonyl groups (O=C–NH2), e.g., amino- and amid-carbonyl functionalization at the edges of GQDs. In the final path (i.e. path (c)) the strong interaction (i.e. dipole moment) between the hydrophilic carbon surface (e.g. phenolic group) and mercury hydration molecules induces the complexation where a single Hg2+ ion bonds with seven water molecules, forming [Hg(H2O)7]2+ complexes [51,53].

The electrochemical deposition of Hg0 from GQD*–Hg2+ complex on the GQD/ITO electrode is depicted in Fig. 5(b). The stepwise redox reaction steps can be expressed in the form of the following sequence [54,55].
(R2)
GQD ∗ –
Hg
2 +
+ 2
e −
↔ GQD ∗ –
Hg 2
2 +
(R3)
GQD ∗ –
Hg 2
2 +
+ 2
e −
↔ GQD ∗ –
2Hg 0
(R4)
2
GQD ∗ –
Hg 2
2 +
↔ 2
GQD ∗ –
Hg 0
+ 2
Hg
2 +

The reaction sequence outlined through (R2)–(R4) elucidates the reduction of Hg2+ onto the GQD/ITO electrode involving the formation of Hg2 2+ as an intermediate or as a disproportionation product. Therefore, the oxygen functional groups on the surface of N-doped GQD electrodes serve as active sites for the adsorption of Hg2+ ions and the graphitic N atoms promotes the charge transfer from the GQD surface to ITO substrate. The rate of nucleation is mainly controlled by the surface energy as well as the GQD/substrate interactions. This ultimately results in a gradual Hg0 accumulation on the GQD electrode surface. At high surface coverage (i.e. high ionic concentration), a lateral interaction between Hg-intermediates becomes more dominant due to strong surface tension of Hg0 (i.e. surface tension: 476 mN m− 1 at 20 °C) and subsequently promotes the Hg0 clustering. Such an undesired clustering hinders the sensitivity of the GQD/ITO hybrid electrodes at high mercury ion concentration. As a result, the N-doped GQD hybrid electrodes maintain high sensitivity at ultralow concentration of the mercury ions, as compared to costly Au-based electrodes [56,57].

The influence of pH value on the current response was investigated within the pH range from 5 to 9, as shown in Fig. S4(a). The pH range selected here is attributed to the fact a weak interaction between N-GQDs and mercury ions through the protonation of N atoms on GQDs at an ultralow pH value, while mercury ions tend to form mercury hydroxide at high pH values. Herein we observe that the current response at pH = 9 is slightly reduced to 85% original one, whereas the current response at pH = 5 is significantly enhanced by 1.2 times, as compared to that at pH = 7. It is believed that the signal change mainly originates from the number of acidic oxygen functionalities (such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) diminished by raising pH value [58,59], thus inhibiting the formation of GQD*–Hg2+ complex, e.g., (R1). Therefore, a decrease in the pH value is beneficial for the adsorption of mercury ions on the N-doped GQDs. The effect of temperature on the current response was examined within the temperature range of 15–35 °C, as illustrated in Fig. S4(b). It can be viewed that the current ratios fall into a small deviation of 2–5%, indicating that the temperature effect is insignificant.

To investigate the stability of the GQD/ITO electrodes, the exhausted electrodes were analyzed with XPS. As shown in Fig. 6(a), XPS Hg 4f peak can be identified at ca. 99–102 eV, whereas O 1s peak confirms the presence of OHg intermediates on the exhausted electrodes (see Fig. 6(b)). Based on Fig. 6(a), predominantly, the mercury is present as Hg0 and Hg2+, indicating the formation of GQD*–Hg0 and GQD*–Hg2+ complexes on the spent N-doped GQD sample upon exposure to the aqueous electrolyte. This observation is in good agreement with the chain reaction mechanism proposed through (R1)–(R4). It is worth noting that the Hg2+ intermediates are the dominant intermediate (> 70%) formed on the electrode nanostructure compared to the Hg0 intermediate. This finding reveals that the GQD*–Hg0 intermediates partially cover the N-doped GQDs (as shown in Fig. 5(b)); thus slightly reducing surface area accessible for the subsequent adsorption of Hg2+ ions. However, the N-doped GQD/ITO electrode still demonstrates superior sensitivity toward mercury ions at ppb level since the GQDs possess vast active surface area as well as strong affinity to the adsorbate.

EIS analysis was employed to probe the impedance distribution (i.e. ohmic, kinetic, mass transport) observed during mercury ions absorption on the N-doped GQD/ITO electrode at various concentrations. Nyquist plots of the electrode, as shown in Fig. 7(a), display a quasi-semicircle with varied arc radius at different Hg2+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the arc radius decreases with increased Hg2+ concentration, implying decreased charge transfer resistance at higher mercury ion concentration. An equivalent circuit model was also proposed to simulate the electrochemical impedance spectra recorded for the N-doped GQD/ITO electrode (see Fig. 7(b)). The equivalent circuit model, as shown in Fig. 7(b), includes three components: (i) high-frequency resistance (R S) for simulating the ohmic losses dominated by the Hg2+-containing electrolyte, (ii) charge transfer resistance (R CT) for modeling the faradaic charge transfer through the electrode/Hg2+ interface, and (iii) constant phase element (Q DL) accounting for the non-faradaic impedance imposed by the electrode/Hg2+ interface. The Z-view software package was used for fitting the impedance spectra. The magnitude of equivalent series resistance (i.e., R ES = R S + R CT) demonstrates a linearly decreasing trend with increased Hg2+ concentration (0–100 ppb). The decreased R ES value can be attributed the formation of GQD*–Hg0 complex that gradually covers the active area of N-doped GQDs at high concentration, decreasing the charge transfer resistance as formulated through the reaction chain (R2)–(R4). Therefore, the equivalent series impedance can indeed serve as a crucial index for assessing the Hg2+ concentration.

Finally, it is important to confirm the selectivity of the sensing mechanism. Selective detection of Hg2+ in NaOH solution is a challenging task due to the presence of other metal ions within the real sample. In this work, the interference by some common metallic ions such as Cd2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ was explored to evaluate the selectivity of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes. Fig. 8(a) shows the electrochemical stripping signals of Hg2+ ions in the presence of other metal ions in the solution. As illustrated in Fig. 8(b), the stripping peak current signal remains relatively unchanged regardless of the type of added metallic ion, i.e. the accuracy of the mercury signal is still >99.2%. Not demonstrating any false signal reveals that the added metal ions do not interfere with the electrochemical detection of Hg2+ ions, resulting in excellent selectivity. The selectivity of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes was also investigated by adding Mg2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cr2+, Ca2+, K+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ metal ions, as illustrated in Fig. S5. The current response on N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes exposed to various metallic ions clearly shows superb selectivity toward Hg2+ ions since there are no significant signal changes in the presence of Mg2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, K+, and Al3+. In the case of Cr2+ and Fe3+, a slight change was observed; that is, the signal ratios are approximately 97.2 and 96.9% after adding Cr2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. Therefore, based on the analysis provided in this work, N-doped GQD hybrid nanoelectrodes can be considered as highly-sensitive, −selective, and durable probes for detecting ultralow concentration of Hg2+ ions within the aqueous solutions.

Through the IR-assisted synthesis technique, N-doped GQDs electrodes were synthesized and used for electrochemically detecting mercury ions within the aqueous solutions. Superior sensitivity and selectivity was achieved toward mercury ions. The IR-assisted method is capable of synthesizing uniform N-doped GQDs with an average particle size of 4.5 nm, through a thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 250 °C. The as-prepared GQDs contained high oxidation and amidation level, i.e. O/C and N/C atomic ratios: 37.6% and 30.7%, respectively. The oxidative and reductive current peaks in the CV analysis demonstrated an increasing trend with increased mercury ion concentration. Also, the equivalent series resistance obtained via EIS analysis decreased with increased Hg2+ concentration. The detection limit of N-doped GQD/ITO electrodes toward Hg2+ ions was as low as 10 ppb with the accumulation time of 32 s. The GQD/ITO electrode also demonstrated superior selectivity toward the target contaminant (i.e. mercury ions). Therefore, the functionalized GQDs synthesized in this work serve as highly-selective/−sensitive sensing probes toward Hg2+ ions within the aqueous solutions. Thus, the robust design of GQD-based electrode can be used for electrochemically sensing Hg2+ contaminants.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Chun-Chieh Fu: Methodology, Visualization, Investigation. Chien-Te Hsieh: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ruey-Shin Juang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology. Siyong Gu: Data curation, Software. Yasser Ashraf Gandomi: Investigation, Validation. Ryan E. Kelly: Data curation. Kenneth D. Kihm: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the support from the Ministry of Science and Technology (Taiwan) under the contracts MOST 108-2221-E-155-036-MY3. Partial financial support for this work under the grant number CMRPD2E0082 by the Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taiwan (Chang Gung Medical Foundation, Taiwan) is deeply appreciated.
